school: Second wife of the Prophet ﷺ, married in Makkah shortly after the death of Khadijah (619 CE), becoming the first woman to carry the title Umm al-Muminin after Khadijah. Her first husband, al-Sakran ibn Amr, had emigrated with her to Abyssinia, where he died. She was a large, jovial woman of great generosity, known for her sharp humor — the Prophet would sometimes laugh until he leaned back. She participated in the farewell pilgrimage. When she grew older, she gave her designated night with the Prophet to Aisha out of love for the Prophet and wishing her to receive what she herself could not provide. The Prophet accepted. Aisha later said: 'I never envied anyone as much as I envied Sawda for her strong character.' She survived the Prophet and died during the caliphate of Muawiyah, approximately 54 AH.
quotes: I gave my night to Aisha — for the companionship of the Prophet in the Hereafter is dearer to me than anything in this world.
I would rather remain in the company of the Prophet than have all the world.

school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers, known throughout the Muslim community as 'Umm al-Masakin' (Mother of the Poor) for her extraordinary generosity to the destitute — a title she carried from before her marriage to the Prophet. She had previously been widowed from Abd Allah ibn Jahsh, who was martyred at Uhud. Her marriage to the Prophet was brief; she died approximately two or three months after the marriage, making her one of only two wives of the Prophet who died during his lifetime (the other being Khadijah). She was buried in Madinah. Her short life is a testament to the Prophet's care for widows of the martyred companions.
quotes: The hand that feeds the hungry has touched something sacred.
Generosity is not a virtue — it is a duty.
The one who gives in secret gives twice.

school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Her marriage to the Prophet was explicitly commanded by Quranic revelation — she had previously been married to Zayd ibn Harithah, the Prophet's adopted son, and after their divorce the Prophet was commanded to marry her to establish that adoption does not carry the same legal weight as biological kinship (Q33:37 — the only verse that mentions a companion by name). She would often boast to the other wives: 'Your families gave you in marriage, but Allah gave me in marriage from above seven heavens.' She was the cousin of the Prophet, from the Banu Asad clan. Known for her deep piety, she would spend entire nights in prayer. Umar once commented that she would spend the whole of Ramadan in prayer and fast. She was extraordinarily generous — when Umar sent her the allowance from the treasury, she distributed it entirely to the poor before the messenger had left. She died in 641 CE / 20 AH and was the first of the Prophet's wives to die after him. The Prophet said of her: 'The quickest of you to join me will be the one with the longest arms' — interpreted as generosity.
quotes: My marriage was arranged not by any human matchmaker but by God Himself, from above the seven heavens.
I am the most honoured of the Prophet’s wives in that my marriage was announced by God.

school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Daughter of al-Harith ibn Abi Dirar, chief of the Banu al-Mustaliq tribe. She was taken captive when the Muslims defeated the Banu al-Mustaliq in 627 CE / 5 AH and was allocated to Thabit ibn Qays as a captive. She negotiated a contract of manumission with Thabit and came to the Prophet to ask for help fulfilling it. He offered to pay her freedom price and marry her. When this news spread among the Sahabah, they said: 'These are now the in-laws of the Prophet' and freed every captive of the Banu al-Mustaliq in their possession — approximately one hundred families in a single day. Aisha remarked: 'I know of no woman who was a greater blessing to her people than Juwayriya.' She was known for constant remembrance of Allah (dhikr) and long prayers. The Prophet once found her still in her prayer spot at midday; he taught her a short formula of dhikr that equaled the reward of the entire morning in speech. She survived the Prophet and died c. 670 CE.
quotes: The Prophet married me, and because of that marriage, a hundred families of my people were freed from captivity — so who has been more blessed than I?
The most beloved words to God are: glory be to God and praise be to Him.

school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Born Ramla, daughter of Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the leader of Quraysh and Islam's greatest opponent at the time. She embraced Islam early and emigrated to Abyssinia with her first husband, Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh. In Abyssinia, her husband converted to Christianity and died there, leaving her widowed in a foreign land. The Prophet sent a proposal of marriage through the Christian king Negus, who accepted on behalf of the Prophet and paid her the marriage gift (mahr) — the Negus himself performed the marriage ceremony in Abyssinia (628 CE), making this the only marriage in Islamic history conducted by proxy, by a non-Muslim king. She returned to Madinah after the Treaty of Hudaybiyya. When her father Abu Sufyan later came to Madinah to renew the treaty, she refused to let him sit on the Prophet's mat, saying: 'It is the Prophet's mat and you are an unclean polytheist.' Her commitment to Islam over family loyalty became legendary. After the conquest of Makkah (630 CE), her father Abu Sufyan finally accepted Islam. She died c. 666 CE.

Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Daughter of Huyayy ibn Akhtab, chief of the Banu Nadir Jewish tribe of Khaybar. After the Muslim victory at Khaybar (628 CE), she came under Muslim protection as a captive. The Prophet gave her the choice of returning to her people or accepting Islam and marriage — she chose Islam. She was known for her intelligence, dignity, and deep devotion. Once, when another wife taunted her with 'You are the daughter of a Jew,' she went to the Prophet in tears; he comforted her: 'You could have answered: My father is Aaron, my uncle is Moses, and my husband is Muhammad — so how are you better than me?' She narrated hadith and was known for her generosity. Her conversion represents the universality of Islam's message — from the tribe of the greatest prophets to being a Mother of the Believers. She died approximately 670 CE.
quotes: I was the daughter of a chief and the granddaughter of a chief — and God chose for me the greatest man to be my husband.
The Prophet said to me: you are the daughter of a prophet, the wife of a prophet, and your uncle was a prophet.

school: The last woman the Prophet ﷺ married, in 629 CE / 7 AH during the Umrat al-Qada (compensatory Umrah). According to Maymuna herself, she offered herself to the Prophet in marriage while he was mounted on his camel — the Quran acknowledges this in Q33:50 (a believing woman who gives herself to the Prophet). She was the sister-in-law of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (Abbas married her sister Umm al-Fadl). Known for her piety and generosity; Aisha remarked that 'she was among the most God-fearing of us and the most attentive to the ties of kinship.' She spent her final years in Saraf near Makkah, at the same location where her marriage contract was concluded, at her own request — she wished to be buried where her marriage was completed. She died approximately 674 CE and is buried in Saraf.
quotes: I was the last of the Prophet’s wives — and I sought nothing from this world except his companionship.
By God, I did not marry the Messenger of God for any worldly reason — I married him for the sake of God.

school: Among the first Muslims to emigrate to Abyssinia (615 CE), fleeing Quraysh persecution under the protection of the Christian king Negus Ashama ibn Abjar.
Among the first Muslims to emigrate to Abyssinia (615 CE), fleeing Quraysh persecution under the protection of the Christian king Negus Ashama ibn Abjar.
Among the earliest Muslims who endured severe persecution and torture at the hands of the Quraysh for refusing to renounce Islam.
Among the earliest Muslims who endured severe persecution and torture at the hands of the Quraysh for refusing to renounce Islam. Died as a martyr (615 CE) at Makkah.
quotes: I will not abandon the religion of Muhammad even if you kill me.
One, One — God is One.

school: Daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. She was betrothed before the Prophet's revelation to Utayba ibn Abi Lahab. When Q111 (Surah al-Masad) was revealed condemning Abu Lahab and his family, Abu Lahab ordered his son to divorce her, and he did so, spitting at her in insult. She emigrated to Madinah after the Hijra. After her sister Ruqayya died (624 CE), the Prophet married her to Uthman ibn Affan — the same companion who had married and lost her sister. This is why Uthman is called 'Dhu al-Nurayn' (Possessor of Two Lights) — he married two daughters of the Prophet. She died 631 CE / 9 AH, in the ninth year of the Hijra, and the Prophet himself helped lower her into her grave.

Eldest daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. She was married to Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi, her maternal cousin, before the Prophet's revelation. When the Prophet declared his mission and she embraced Islam, her husband Abu al-As initially remained on the Quraysh side. At the Battle of Badr, Abu al-As was captured by the Muslims; Zaynab sent her own mother's necklace as ransom — when the Prophet saw it, his eyes filled with tears and he asked the companions if they would free Abu al-As without ransom as a favour to Zaynab. They agreed. Abu al-As kept his word, sending Zaynab to Madinah; she emigrated alone under Quraysh harassment, suffering a miscarriage during her journey due to the attack of Habbar ibn al-Aswad. The Prophet wept at her arrival and prayed against those who had attacked her. Abu al-As later accepted Islam (627 CE) and was reunited with Zaynab. She died 629 CE / 8 AH.

Daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah al-Zahra, and granddaughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She is among the most venerated women in Islamic history for her role at and after the Battle of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH). She witnessed the massacre of her brother Husayn and 72 companions, the deaths of her sons and nephews, and was taken as a prisoner by Yazid ibn Muawiyah to Damascus. In Yazid's court, she delivered one of the most powerful speeches in Arabic oratory — denouncing the tyranny of Yazid to his face, before his entire court, despite being in captivity. She refused humiliation, declaring: 'Do you think you have closed the gates of heaven on us? You have not. You have killed the bodies of noble men, but you cannot kill what they stood for.' Her speech turned public opinion and preserved the narrative of Karbala for all time. She is called 'Aqila Banu Hashim' (the Wise Woman of the House of the Prophet). She returned to Madinah and later moved to Egypt or Syria, where she died c. 682 CE.
quotes: O Yazid — scheme whatever you wish, strive whatever you will — by God you will never erase our memory.
Our dignity cannot be taken by oppression — it is given by God and no tyrant can remove it.
I saw nothing but beauty in what God decreed for us.

school: Daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah al-Zahra, and granddaughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She was present at the Battle of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH) alongside her sister Zaynab bint Ali and witnessed the martyrdom of their brother Husayn and the companions. After Karbala she was taken as a prisoner alongside her sister to Kufa and then Damascus. She narrated hadith and is cited in classical sources. She is buried in Damascus according to one tradition, though other accounts differ.
quotes: They silenced the men — but they forgot that the women were listening.
The witness who speaks the truth when no one else will has fulfilled the greatest duty.
Injustice does not end when the oppressor wins — it ends when the oppressed finds their voice.

school: Daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. Like her sister Umm Kulthum, she was betrothed before the revelation — to Utba ibn Abi Lahab — and similarly divorced by him when Q111 was revealed. She emigrated to Abyssinia twice with her husband Uthman ibn Affan. She returned to Madinah ill and worsened during the campaign of Badr. She died in Madinah on the very day the news of the victory at Badr reached the city (624 CE / 2 AH); the Prophet was absent at Badr and returned to find her already buried. According to tradition, she was the first Muslim to be buried in Baqi al-Gharqad, the main cemetery of Madinah. Her death prompted the Prophet to say: 'Join our predecessor Uthman ibn Maz'un.' Uthman ibn Affan later married her sister Umm Kulthum.
Among the earliest Muslims who endured severe persecution and torture at the hands of the Quraysh for refusing to renounce Islam.

Among the most revered women in Islamic tradition — she was both the biological mother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and the foster mother who raised Prophet Muhammad ﷺ after his mother Aminah died when he was six years old. She was the wife of Abu Talib, making her the Prophet's aunt by marriage. She embraced Islam and is counted among the early Companions. The Prophet honoured her with extraordinary devotion throughout his life. When she died (626 CE / 5 AH), the Prophet gave her his own shirt as a burial shroud, prayed her funeral prayer himself — standing four takbirs, the maximum — and lay in her grave before burial so that the grave would be sanctified for her. He said: 'She treated me like her own children, always giving them lesser food so I could eat better.' In Sufi tradition, she holds the highest feminine status as the mother of both the Prophet's rearing and the Imam of walaya. She is buried in Madinah.
quotes: I raised Ali as my own son and Muhammad as the son of my heart — there was never a nobler household.
The Messenger of God honoured me more than any of my own sons ever could.

school: Paternal aunt of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, daughter of Abd al-Muttalib, and full sister of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib. She was among the earliest to embrace Islam and participated in the Battle of Uhud (625 CE) — accounts record her defending the Prophet with a spear when the battle turned against the Muslims, striking at those approaching the Prophet's position. She composed powerful elegies (marathi) for Hamza after his martyrdom at Uhud, which the Prophet heard and wept at. She was also a poet, composing Arabic verse that is preserved in classical sources. She narrated a number of hadith. She is said to have been the first woman to kill an enemy in battle for Islam — according to Ibn Sa'd, she killed a spy who had infiltrated the Muslim encampment at the Trench. She died approximately 641 CE / 20 AH.

Companion (Sahabi/Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. A freed slave who embraced Islam, demonstrating the Prophet's teaching of human equality. A narrator of hadith, preserving prophetic traditions for subsequent generations. Also participated in the battle(s) of Khaybar.

Daughter of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the Lion of Allah) and therefore a first cousin of the Prophet ﷺ. After her father's martyrdom at Uhud, she was in Makkah. A famous incident occurred: both Ali ibn Abi Talib and Zayd ibn Harithah requested custody of her after Hamza's death. The Prophet awarded her to Jafar ibn Abi Talib, saying: 'The maternal aunt is like the mother.' She is among the female companions.

Daughter of Abu Talib and elder sister of Ali ibn Abi Talib, and thus the cousin of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She is notable for two things: the Prophet famously began his Night Journey (Isra) from her house in Makkah according to one narration, and she is recorded in classical sources as having prayed the Fajr prayer with the Prophet after his return, making her a direct witness to the event. When the Makkans besieged her home after the Conquest, she gave sanctuary to two Qurayshi men; the Prophet said: 'We give protection to those you have given protection, Umm Hani.' She refused the Prophet's earlier proposal of marriage (when young) due to her existing marriage. She narrated approximately 46 hadith. She was widowed and lived in Makkah. Her brother Ali and she maintained close ties throughout their lives.
quotes: I gave protection to those who asked — for mercy is not weakness.
The one who shows kindness to the stranger has understood the message of the Prophet.
God honours those who honour His creation.

school: Paternal aunt of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, daughter of Abd al-Muttalib. Among the aunts of the Prophet who embraced Islam. Her son Tulayb ibn Umayr was an early Muslim. She composed elegies (marathi) and was known for her poetic talent — a trait shared among the women of Banu Hashim. Narrated a small number of hadith.

Daughter of al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib, cousin of the Prophet ﷺ. She embraced Islam and narrated hadith. A famous hadith is attributed to her: the Prophet told her that she could make a condition in her Hajj intention due to her illness — which established the juristic principle of conditional Hajr intention (ishtirat). Married al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad.
Companion from the Quraysh tribe of Makkah who embraced Islam. A narrator of hadith, preserving prophetic traditions for subsequent generations. Also participated in the battle(s) of Khaybar.
Companion from the Quraysh tribe of Makkah who embraced Islam. A narrator of hadith, preserving prophetic traditions for subsequent generations. Also participated in the battle(s) of Khaybar.

Paternal aunt of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, daughter of Abd al-Muttalib. According to some traditions, she had a prophetic dream three days before the Battle of Badr which foretold the defeat of Quraysh, which she shared and which caused alarm in Makkah. She embraced Islam and is counted among the companion women who narrated hadith.
Companion from the Quraysh tribe of Makkah who embraced Islam. A narrator of hadith, preserving prophetic traditions for subsequent generations.

school: Companion from the Quraysh tribe of Makkah who embraced Islam. A narrator of hadith, preserving prophetic traditions for subsequent generations.

Member of the Banu Hashim clan and companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Counted among the Sahaba by classical biographers including Ibn Sa'd. Limited biographical detail has been preserved in the primary sources.

The greatest female poet in the Arabic literary tradition, widely considered the most gifted elegist (rathiyya poet) in all of Arabic literature, male or female. She composed devastating elegies for her brothers Sakhr and Muawiyah who died in pre-Islamic tribal wars — poems so powerful they set the standard for Arabic grief poetry for centuries. She accepted Islam and came to the Prophet ﷺ, who listened to her poetry and praised it, saying: 'More, Khansa, more!' — she recited standing before him in the mosque. At the Battle of al-Qadisiyya (636 CE), she sent her four sons to fight the Persian forces. When news came that all four had been martyred in a single day, she said: 'Praise be to Allah who honoured me with their martyrdom. I hope Allah will reunite me with them in His mercy.' Her composure in grief stunned the companions. She composed an elegy for all four. She died in Madinah c. 646 CE, the last of the great elegists.
quotes: Weep — for he who is worth weeping for deserves every tear.
Patience does not mean that grief does not hurt — it means you bear it with God’s name on your lips.
The noble man is one whose death leaves a wound in the world.

school: Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.

Ansari companion and one of the most important female narrators of prophetic hadith. She participated in six military campaigns with the Prophet ﷺ, treating the wounded and preparing food — her presence on the battlefield was so valued that she is cited by name in the sources. She is the primary female authority on the Islamic laws of washing and preparing the dead for burial (ghusl al-mayyit); she personally washed the Prophet's daughter Zaynab bint Muhammad at his direction, and he gave her detailed instructions which she preserved and transmitted. Her narrations on menstruation, funeral rites, and women's religious obligations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence on these topics. She narrated hadith cited in all six major canonical collections. She later lived in Basra where she taught and transmitted hadith until her death.
Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. Her hadith are cited in Abu Dawud. Lived in Makkah.

One of the most dramatic conversion stories in Islamic history. Before Islam she was one of the Prophet's fiercest opponents — she incited the Battle of Uhud, hired Wahshi to kill Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the Prophet's uncle), and mutilated Hamza's body after his death, an act she later deeply regretted. She was the wife of Abu Sufyan, leader of Quraysh. She accepted Islam on the day of the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE), coming to the Prophet ﷺ in disguise. When she revealed herself and pledged allegiance, the Prophet accepted her Islam. She thereafter became a sincere Muslim, narrating hadith and participating in the community. She fought at the Battle of Yarmouk (636 CE) against the Byzantine army, rallying the Muslim fighters. She is the mother of Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. Her story stands as evidence of Islam's willingness to accept any soul, regardless of past hostility.
quotes: By God, there was no household on earth I wished to see humiliated more than yours — but now there is no household I wish to see honoured more than yours.
I was your worst enemy — and now I am your sincerest follower.

school: Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. Her hadith are cited in all six major collections. Lived in Makkah.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh clan of Banu 'adi. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.

Among the seventy-five men and two women who took the Second Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE / before the Hijra), the founding constitutional moment of the Islamic state in Madinah. The Prophet secretly met this delegation from Madinah at Mina during Hajj and they swore to protect him as they would protect their own families. Participated in: Uhud, Khaybar. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh clan of Banu makhzum. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.

Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. Allah honoured all who were present by name in the Quran: 'Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts' (Q48:18). Being of Ridwan is one of the highest designations a companion could hold. Recognised for scholarship in Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh). Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. Participated in: Uhud. From the Ansar of Madinah. Her hadith are cited in Bukhari. Present at Uhud. Lived in Medinah.
quotes: I married Abu Talha, and my dowry was Islam — for what dowry could be better than faith?
O Messenger of God, pray that God grants me what I have asked — the garden of Paradise.

school: Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh clan of Banu 'abd shams. Her hadith are cited in Tirmidhi. Lived in Makkah.
Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh clan of Banu 'adi. Her hadith are cited in Tirmidhi. Lived in Makkah.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.
Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. Died 667 CE in Medinah. Her hadith are cited in Abu Dawud. Died 667 CE in Medinah.
Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. Her hadith are cited in Muslim. Lived in Abyssinia.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Ansar of Madinah. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Medinah.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh clan of Banu 'adi. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, recognised as a scholar and authority in Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh). She was consulted on legal rulings by the companions and successors. Known for her rulings on matters of Islamic jurisprudence — women and men came to her with questions, and her opinions are recorded in the classical hadith compilations. Her hadith are cited in Ibn Majah. Lived in Makkah.
quotes: Patience is not the absence of grief — it is grief carried with trust in God.
I served the Prophet from his birth — and that is more than enough for any life.

school: Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Ansar of Madinah. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Medina.

Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. Died as a martyr (648 CE) at Cyprus. From the Ansar of Madinah. Her hadith are cited in Bukhari. Died as a martyr (648 CE at Cyprus).
Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Ansar of Madinah. Her hadith are cited in Abu Dawud. Lived in Medinah.

quotes: I have lived on the lip of insanity, wanting to know reasons, knocking on a door. It opens. I have been knocking from the inside.

Companion from Yemen and the mother-in-law of the Prophet ﷺ — her daughter Maymuna bint al-Harith was the Prophet's last wife. She was also the mother of Umm al-Fadl (wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib) and Asma bint Umays (wife of Jafar ibn Abi Talib). The Prophet ﷺ said of her: 'Hind bint Awf is the most honoured of mothers-in-law' — she had three daughters who were all wives of three of the most important men in early Islam simultaneously. She emigrated to Madinah and narrated hadith. She was known for her nobility and character.

Companion (Sahabiyya) and sister of the famous Asma bint Umays (who is in the database). She was the wife of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Lion of Allah, martyred at Uhud. As Hamza's widow she witnessed the greatest bereavement the early Muslim community suffered after Badr. She later married Shaddad ibn al-Hadi. She narrated hadith from the Prophet ﷺ and preserved several traditions about Hamza and the early community. Her family — the daughters of Umays — were among the most distinguished women in early Islam, with three sisters marrying three of the most important companions.

One of the most distinguished female scholars in early Islamic history and the leading female religious authority of Damascus. She studied directly under Abu Darda, the great companion and judge of Damascus (her husband), and under Abu Hurairah. She narrated extensively from the Prophet ﷺ and from the major companions. What makes her exceptional is that she taught in the mosque alongside men — the Caliph Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan himself sat in her teaching circle as a student. She is reported to have said: 'I have sought worship in everything and have found nothing more pleasing to me than sitting with scholars discussing knowledge.' She was consulted on legal rulings by the leading jurists of Syria. Her student Makhul al-Shami — himself a major jurist — transmitted her knowledge. She represents the robust tradition of female scholarship that characterised Damascus in the first Islamic century.
quotes: I sought knowledge as the men sought it — and I found that God does not distinguish between a male seeker and a female seeker.
I have tried all forms of worship — and I found nothing more nourishing than sitting with scholars.

school: Companion of the Prophet ﷺ and mother of Aisha bint Abi Bakr (Mother of the Believers) and Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr. She was the wife of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. She emigrated with her family to Madinah in the Hijra. She was present during the incident of the Ifk (the slander against Aisha) and witnessed her daughter's distress. When Aisha fell ill during the slander period, Umm Ruman nursed her. The Prophet ﷺ visited her home frequently. She died during the Prophet's lifetime — he descended into her grave himself and prayed for her, saying: 'Whoever wishes to see a woman of the houris (Paradise) let them look at Umm Ruman.' She is buried in Madinah.

Ansari companion who memorised the entire Quran (hafiz) during the Prophet's lifetime — rare for anyone, male or female, at that time. She asked the Prophet for permission to go to the Battle of Badr to nurse the wounded; he permitted her. The Prophet ﷺ himself came to her house to visit her and appointed her to lead the prayer for her household — one of the earliest documented cases of a woman being appointed as imam in Islamic history. The Prophet called her 'al-Shahida' (the martyr) before her death, prophesying her fate. She was later murdered by her own servants (who wanted their freedom), making her a martyr as the Prophet had foretold. Her story is cited in discussions of women's religious leadership in classical jurisprudence.
quotes: The Prophet gave me permission to lead my household in prayer — and I was honoured to carry the Quran in my heart.
I wished to be a martyr, and the Prophet called me the Martyr.

school: Also known as Umm al-Fadl, wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ. She is considered by many classical scholars to have been the second woman to accept Islam after Khadijah — some place her equal with Khadijah in priority. She narrated directly from the Prophet and her hadith appear in all major canonical collections. She wielded considerable influence in the early community due to her husband's position as the Prophet's uncle and later as the patriarch of the Banu Hashim after the Prophet's death. She was the mother of several important figures including Abdullah ibn Abbas, the greatest Quran scholar among the companions.
quotes: I was the second woman to accept Islam after Khadijah — and I have never regretted that decision.
Faith entered my heart before it entered the hearts of many men.

Wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (uncle of the Prophet) and one of the earliest women to accept Islam — according to some accounts, the second woman after Khadijah to enter the faith. She was the maternal aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid. She narrated several important hadith directly from the Prophet ﷺ, including the definitive ruling on fasting on the Day of Arafat — when people disputed whether the Prophet was fasting, she sent him milk to drink in front of everyone, settling the question. She is also the narrator of a famous hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ showed her how to raise her hands in supplication. Her narrations are in the major canonical collections. She was beloved by the Prophet, who said: 'Umm al-Fadl and Khadijah are the best women.'

Female emigrant (Muhajirat) from the Banu Makhzum of Makkah who made the Hijra to Madinah. The Muhajirat left behind homes, property and family in Makkah for the sake of their faith — their Hijra is as complete and meritorious as their husbands'. The Quran addresses them directly: 'Their Lord responded to them: I will not let the deeds of any doer among you go to waste, male or female' (Q3:195).
